lecture_context.lecture_title: Lecture 16: Vision and Language — Multi-Modal Foundation Models

unit.name: From task-specific models to foundation models
unit.description: Introduces the lecture theme and contrasts specialized computer vision models with the foundation-model paradigm.
unit.summary: The lecture opens by framing multimodal foundation models as a shift away from building one specialized model per dataset and task. Traditional workflows collect training and test sets, train a task-specific model such as an image classifier or captioner, and evaluate it on that task [ts=59s]. Foundation models instead pre-train on broad skills and data, then adapt to individual tasks through fine-tuning, few-shot use, or even zero-shot use with little or no new training data [ts=98s] [ts=134s]. The lecturer lists the agenda: image-classification foundation models such as CLIP and CoCa, models that combine language models with vision models, models with non-text outputs such as masks or images, and chaining multiple models together [ts=172s] [ts=208s]. A practical definition is given: foundation models are generally robust across many tasks and often have many parameters, large-scale data, and self-supervised pretraining objectives [ts=226s].

key_points.text: Traditional course models were framed as specialized systems trained and evaluated for individual tasks.
A foundation model is pre-trained on broad skills and later adapted to task-specific needs.
Foundation models can often adapt with very little data or no task-specific training data.
The lecture focuses on multimodal vision foundation models, including CLIP, CoCa, language-plus-vision models, mask/image outputs, and chaining.
Typical foundation-model traits include robustness across tasks, many parameters, large training datasets, and self-supervised objectives.

kp_catalog.name: Foundation-model pretraining and adaptation paradigm
kp_catalog.description: A foundation model is pretrained broadly and then adapted to many downstream tasks through fine-tuning, prompting, zero-shot, or few-shot use. The core recipe is broad pretraining followed by task-directed post-training or adaptation.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because foundation-model pretraining and adaptation paradigm formalizes how a foundation model is pretrained broadly and then adapted to many downstream tasks through fine-tuning, prompting, zero-shot, or few-shot use, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while importance labels split between critical and high.

kp_catalog.name: Traits of foundation models
kp_catalog.description: Foundation models are typically general and robust across tasks, and often use large parameter counts, large datasets, and self-supervised objectives.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because traits of foundation models clarifies foundation models are typically general and robust across tasks, and often use large parameter counts, large datasets, and self-supervised objectives, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
